A Bill of Materials (BOM) is a comprehensive list of all the raw materials, components, and assemblies required to manufacture a product. It includes the quantities of each material needed and may also detail other aspects like the labor required, tools, and instructions for manufacturing.

Components of a BOM:
Part Number/Item Code: Unique identifier for each component.
Description: A brief description of the component or material.
Quantity: The amount of each component required for manufacturing.
Unit of Measure: How the quantity is measured (e.g., pieces, kilograms).
Cost: The cost of each component or material.
Supplier Information: Details about where the material is sourced.
Assembly Instructions: Steps or methods required to assemble the product.
Lead Times: Time required to procure or manufacture each item.
Accounting Workflow: Item Receipt + Converting to Bills and Customer Invoices
Purchase Order (PO) Creation:

You begin by creating a Purchase Order for the materials listed in the BOM. This document is sent to your supplier, detailing the items, quantities, and agreed prices.
Receiving the Items (Item Receipt):

When the materials arrive, you create an Item Receipt in your accounting or inventory management system. This document acknowledges that you have received the goods but haven’t yet paid for them.
The inventory is updated to reflect the newly received items, increasing the stock levels.
Converting Item Receipt to Bill:

After confirming the receipt of items, the Item Receipt is converted into a Bill.
The Bill reflects your obligation to pay the supplier. It records the amounts owed and the due date for payment.
This step often includes matching the Bill to the original Purchase Order to ensure consistency in quantities and prices.
Paying the Bill:

The Bill is paid according to the payment terms agreed upon with the supplier. This transaction reduces your accounts payable and the cash or bank balance.
Manufacturing the Product:

Once all necessary materials are in stock (as per the BOM), the production process begins. During manufacturing, the materials are used up, and their costs are transferred from inventory to work-in-progress (WIP) or directly to the cost of goods sold (COGS), depending on your accounting system.
Creating Customer Invoices:

After the product is manufactured and ready for delivery, you generate a Customer Invoice.
This invoice includes the final product details, quantities, and prices, reflecting what the customer owes you.
The invoice is sent to the customer, and the amount is recorded as accounts receivable.

A Bill of Lading (BOL) is a legal document issued by a carrier to a shipper that serves as a receipt for the goods being shipped, a contract of carriage, and a title for the goods. It includes details like the type, quantity, and destination of the goods, and it must accompany the goods being transported. The BOL is crucial in the shipping process as it is used to ensure that the carrier delivers the goods to the correct location and to the correct party. It can also be used in disputes over shipping, as it serves as proof that the carrier received the goods as described.

A Packing Slip, on the other hand, is a document that details the contents of a shipment. It lists the items included in the shipment, but it doesn't serve as a legal contract or title for the goods. A packing slip is primarily used by the recipient to check that all items listed have been received and to verify the quantity and description of the products.

Key Differences:
Purpose:

Bill of Lading: Acts as a legal contract, a receipt, and a title for the goods.
Packing Slip: Lists the contents of the shipment for verification purposes.
Legal Status:

Bill of Lading: Legally binding document that outlines the terms of the transportation contract.
Packing Slip: Not legally binding; purely informational.
Use Case:

Bill of Lading: Used by the shipper, carrier, and consignee to manage the shipping process.
Packing Slip: Used by the consignee to ensure all items have been received.
Contents:

Bill of Lading: Includes detailed shipping information like the shipper, carrier, consignee, description of goods, and delivery instructions.
Packing Slip: Includes a list of items, quantities, and sometimes item prices or codes, but not detailed shipping or contract information.
In summary, while both documents are important in the shipping process, the Bill of Lading has a broader legal and logistical role, whereas the Packing Slip is focused on item verification.

Purpose and Focus
Inspection App: Primarily designed for verification and compliance, focusing on systematically assessing conditions, equipment, and standards. For example, a fire safety inspection app would allow inspectors to check fire alarms, sprinklers, extinguishers, and evacuation routes, ensuring they comply with regulations and function as intended.

Workflow and Usage
Inspection App: Inspections follow a structured, checklist-based format where items are assessed, marked as “pass/fail,” and documented for compliance. Users typically log details such as dates, location, condition, and photos, ensuring that every element meets safety codes.


Data Tracking and Reporting
Inspection App: Emphasizes data collection, trend analysis, and compliance reporting. Reports summarize the status of inspections, identify recurring issues, and highlight areas needing improvement.

Inspection vs. Work order
Use an Inspection App if your primary need is ensuring compliance, assessing risks, or adhering to regulatory requirements. This app is ideal for scheduled assessments of equipment or environments, ensuring everything is up to code.
Use a Work Order App if your goal is to manage and track repair and maintenance tasks efficiently. This app is best for prioritizing, assigning, and tracking corrective actions arising from inspection reports or other triggers.

A Warehouse Management System (WMS) is a software solution designed to optimize and manage warehouse operations, from receiving goods and managing inventory to order fulfillment and shipping. While basic inventory tracking usually handles stock quantities and product locations, a WMS offers much broader functionality.

Key Differences between a WMS and Basic Inventory Tracking:
Scope of Operations:

Inventory Tracking: Focuses on the simple recording of stock levels, item locations, and maybe reorder points.
WMS: Manages the entire lifecycle of warehouse operations, including receiving, storage, picking, packing, shipping, and even labor tracking.
Automation and Efficiency:

Inventory Tracking: Manually updates stock levels and locations based on transactions.
WMS: Automates tasks such as barcode scanning, real-time inventory updates, task prioritization, and optimization of warehouse layout and picking routes.
Order Fulfillment and Shipping:

Inventory Tracking: Records when stock is depleted through sales or shipping.
WMS: Directly manages the order picking, packing, and shipping process, often interfacing with shipping carriers and managing complex multi-order processes.
Complexity and Customization:

Inventory Tracking: Suited for simple environments with less variation in stock movements.
WMS: Designed for large, complex warehouses handling multiple SKUs, bulk orders, and requiring sophisticated workflows, such as batch picking, wave picking, and cross-docking.
Integration with Hardware:

Inventory Tracking: Limited interaction with hardware.
WMS: Interfaces with warehouse hardware such as barcode scanners, RFID readers, conveyor belts, and automated picking systems.
Integration with QuickBooks: End-to-End Workflow Perspective
Inventory Syncing:

The WMS syncs inventory levels with QuickBooks in real-time. This ensures that both systems reflect accurate stock levels, preventing over-ordering or stockouts.
Purchase Order and Receiving:

When a Purchase Order (PO) is created in QuickBooks, it’s pushed to the WMS. The WMS manages the receiving process, ensuring the correct items and quantities are received. Once received, it sends a confirmation to QuickBooks, updating the inventory and marking the PO as fulfilled.
Sales Order Fulfillment:

When a sales order is created in QuickBooks, it’s sent to the WMS. The WMS allocates inventory, generates picking lists, and manages the fulfillment process. Once an order is fulfilled, it updates QuickBooks to reflect the shipped order and adjusts inventory accordingly.
Shipping and Invoicing:

The WMS can integrate with third-party shipping solutions, automatically calculating shipping costs and printing labels. Once the item is shipped, the information is sent back to QuickBooks, which can trigger invoice creation and customer notification.
Returns and Reverse Logistics:

When products are returned, the WMS manages the process, including inspection, restocking, and inventory adjustments. The return is also reflected in QuickBooks, ensuring accurate financial and stock updates.
Reporting and Reconciliation:

Both systems share real-time data, ensuring that financial reports, inventory values, and costs of goods sold (COGS) are accurately represented. The integration allows seamless reconciliation between physical inventory and financial records.
In summary, a WMS provides an advanced solution that goes beyond simple inventory tracking, focusing on optimizing warehouse operations. Integration with QuickBooks creates an efficient end-to-end process, streamlining inventory management, order fulfillment, and financial tracking.

Summary:
Request to implement the ability to create and manage bundled items directly in Method:CRM, mirroring QuickBooks functionality. Bundled items are groups of products or services sold together as a single unit, commonly used in QuickBooks for streamlined invoicing and inventory management. Currently, Method only supports viewing bundled items synced from QuickBooks, not creating them.

Objective:
Allow users to create and manage bundled items within Method:CRM, ensuring a seamless workflow for businesses that sell grouped products or services.

Proposed Features:

Bundled Item Creation:

Introduce an interface for creating bundled items in Method.
Allow users to select and group multiple existing products or services into a single bundled item.
Customization Options:

Enable users to name the bundle, set a default price, and optionally define quantities for each component.
Allow optional visibility of component items on invoices (e.g., show/hide individual components).
Sync with QuickBooks:

Ensure that bundled items created in Method sync seamlessly with QuickBooks.
Maintain compatibility with existing bundled items synced from QuickBooks.
Bundled Item Management:

Allow editing or deleting of existing bundled items within Method.
Provide an overview of the bundles and their components for better inventory management.
Reporting and Insights:

Include bundled items in sales, inventory, and financial reports.
Track sales and performance of bundles alongside individual items.
Benefits:

Streamlined Workflow: Businesses can manage their entire inventory system in Method without relying on QuickBooks for creating bundled items.
Enhanced Flexibility: Allows customization and visibility options that better align with diverse business needs.
Seamless Integration: Maintains sync with QuickBooks to ensure consistency between platforms.
Improved User Experience: Simplifies invoicing and inventory processes for businesses selling bundled products or services.

Reason: Mobility City is looking to utilize the mapping widget and would like the ability to click on a location and see details about the location that is clicked

Use Case: By having Data Point Clicks, we can display information for the location that is clicked. Eg. For Work Orders, if we clicked on a Work Order, we might be able to the Items related to the Work Order so the techs have an idea of what to expect without having to go back and forth between each different work order.

Customer does not want their individual pricing for items parts to show on invoices and items. They have protected pricing with our vendors and would like to make sure that it isn't shared with other parties. They just want to show the item, quantity and total they ordered and not individual pricing for parts they purchase.
This customer would like to have a space to add attachments to his Items so that he can add information about the technical data sheet of his products.

User has many items with lengthy item descriptions which often go over the 255-character limit on the Lineitem Grid. 

Can the 255-character limit by increased?
User would like to track Location on all transactions at the lineitems level

Customer would like to change logic controlling estimate screens update.

Customer would like the subtotal to be more dynamic and update on any change.

The Dynamic nature of being able to update is important so we can keep track of totals as items are added. Rather than adding items then pressing save to know the total.
User is in the trophy business and would like an invoice report based on items category.
The customer would like to know if there is another alternative to adding group items in Method. They know they can add bundle items in QBO but want to be able to do it in Method.

User is in the beverage and coffee business and has fields crews that deliver consumables on a regular basis. 

The Work order generally starts with a list of items that the customer likes to buy, all with a quantity of 0. 

Instead of going into each item to make the change on quantity, the customer is wondering if we can use an editable grid to make changes on one screen, saving their drivers valuable time on each delivery.

User is in auto part sales and has a huge quantity of inventory itsems and parts numbers for the item name. 

They are often face-to-face with customers for order processing and find our open line-item search difficult to use, (they get a lot of returns).

They are wondering if we can restrict the search utiliity to be Item name only plus query based on the sequential order I.e 123 should only return items that start with 123.

User is wondering if its possible to Batch-update all Taxes on the Lineitems to Tax or Non-Tax. 

Ive made recommendations that the customer tax code is a better option to accomplish this but curious if such a customization exist.

The “Group ID”, field is currently a text field, want to make this a dropdown field where you choose an option from a provided list instead of this being a manual entry field. (See attachment)

This will keep the group # consistent across all items and prevent the same group #’s from being entered in numerous different ways (for example Group 11A / Group 11-A / 11A / group 11a etc.) 

It should have a screen to create groups for this field as necessary.

1. Making line items grid available on New/Edit Opportunity screen. This way, sales reps can enter list of items that they are selling to the customer. 

2. Opportunity Amount will be auto-populated and reflect the total amount of every line items selected.

Duplicating a table is really helpful for a couple of reasons:

1.) It enables me to create a table that matches the schema of another table for importing.
- For example, importing into the activity table - Ideally I would just like to copy the activity table
- Convert all the fields to “plain text” so I can import into this “Staging Table”

Then use actions to import the data.

2.) In general this does help us create apps / tables that share similar structures but keep the data separate. 
- For example, an Inspection List vs. an Installation List. 

The same information is captured; however, for a number of reasons, it’s easier to manage this information in two separate places vs. reporting on it all in the same place. 

3.) Keeping records of historical changes
- For example, a packing slip on a Sales Order
- As things are shipped out, we may want to keep track of items that are shipping.

QB enables us to keep track of how many are shipping up until today.
However, we might want to keep a log of shipping records, this is especially helpful for partial shipments. 

Saves customizers hours of creating tables and schemas.

Based of the customer's last invoice, ability to quickly see what the last product they purchased on the Contacts Grid.

This client for example provides classes, so their items are the courses. They want to be able to see on the contacts grid what the last course a particular customer had purchased.

Customer workflow involves making and selling new items frequently and would like the ability for their team to create a new item from the estimate app if the item does not already exist.

QuickBooks is able to do this inline when creating a new transaction.

I found that if I were to make a Sales Receipt for a Customer, it will appear as a Sales Receipt and a Donation in the Contact’s App Ribbon. 

I made sure to use Items that are not set as a Donation Item in the Donation’s Preferences and the Sales Receipts still appeared as a Donation.
